serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Bhindi Do Pyaza is approximately 250 g, typically presented in a bowl as a side dish alongside roti, naan, or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Okra (Bhindi) cooked dish, adapted with onion-tomato gravy composition
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Bhindi Do Pyaza in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked okra (bhindi) data combined with USDA FoodData Central data for cooked onion and tomato in proportion consistent with the dish composition (approximately 50% okra, 30% onion, 10% tomato, 10% oil and spices). Input record values (energy 128 kcal, protein 3.2 g, fat 8.8 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g, dietary fibre 3.2 g, sodium 260 mg, sugar 2.8 g, saturated fat 1.2 g, calcium 40 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 0.0 mg, trans fat 0.0 g, vitamin C 12 mg) are consistent with this composition estimate and are retained. Cholesterol is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. Trans fat is confirmed zero as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for okra-based vegetable curry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bhindi Do Pyaza. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed vegetable curry (GI ~40–45) and the known low GI of okra (GI ~20) and onion (GI ~10). The high dietary fibre content (3.2 g/100g) from okra's beta-glucan and mucilage further supports a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 25.0) / 100 = 10.0, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 10.0 × 2.5 = 25.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 260 mg/100g (650 mg per 250 g serving) is borderline for hypertension management; potassium from okra and onion partially offsets this.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to 150–200 g to keep sodium intake below 400 mg per meal contribution; the dish's high potassium (~750 mg per serving) and dietary fibre (8 g per serving) provide compensatory cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) driven by okra's beta-glucan and mucilage fibre, which slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption; carbohydrate is modest at 10 g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bhindi Do Pyaza is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI (~40), high dietary fibre (3.2 g/100g), and low sugar (2.8 g/100g); pair with a small portion of whole-grain roti rather than white rice to maintain a low overall glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (1.2 g/100g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, and high dietary fibre (3.2 g/100g) support a favourable cardiovascular lipid profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is appropriate for cardiovascular disease patients given its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and fibre-rich okra content which supports LDL reduction; sodium at 260 mg/100g should be monitored and cooking oil quantity minimised to reduce total fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains nightshade vegetables (tomato, green chilli) which may trigger solanine-mediated joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients with confirmed nightshade sensitivity should avoid or request preparation without tomato and green chilli; if nightshade tolerance is established, the anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric, ginger, garlic, and okra's beta-glucan fibre provide meaningful benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bhindi Do Pyaza (भिंडी दो प्याज़ा) is a classic North Indian vegetarian dry curry featuring okra (bhindi) as the primary ingredient, cooked with a generous double quantity of onions — the meaning of 'do pyaza' in Hindi. The dish is prepared by sautéing whole or sliced okra in oil until the characteristic sliminess is reduced, then combining it with a spiced onion-tomato masala made from cumin seeds, turmeric, coriander powder, red chilli powder, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and green chilli, finished with amchur (dried mango powder) for tartness. The texture is tender-firm with slightly caramelised onion and a dry-to-semi-dry gravy. The flavour profile is earthy, mildly spiced, and tangy. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 128 kcal, 3.2 g protein, 8.8 g fat (saturated fat only 1.2 g), 10.0 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g dietary fibre, and 260 mg sodium. Okra contributes beta-glucan and mucilage fibre that slows glucose absorption, making this dish suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management (GI ~40, low). The dish is entirely plant-based — vegan, vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free — with zero cholesterol and zero trans fat. It is served as a side dish in North Indian, Punjabi, and restaurant Indian cuisine contexts, typically alongside roti, naan, paratha, or steamed rice. In Singapore, it is commonly available at North Indian restaurant chains. Clinically, the high fibre and low GI make it beneficial for glycaemic control, while the low saturated fat and zero cholesterol support cardiovascular health. Sodium at 260 mg/100g warrants monitoring for hypertension patients.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bhindi Do Pyaza exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked okra data and USDA FoodData Central cooked onion and tomato data in proportions consistent with the dish composition. The input record values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed vegetable curry. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.

Potassium estimated at 300 mg/100g based on ICMR-NIN okra and USDA onion/tomato data; this is an estimate and should be verified if precise potassium data is required. 6. This dish is served at Curry Village — Authentic North Indian Cuisine, Boat Quay, Singapore, as listed on GrabFood. 9. Multilingual names: Chinese names are descriptive translations (秋葵双葱咖喱 = okra double-onion curry); Malay name is a descriptive translation; Tamil name is a transliteration; Hindi name is the native-script canonical form.
